input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) characterized by the following key attributes: **(1) Multi-layered abstraction**: The system is modeled across **four hierarchical levels**—**requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer**—to manage complexity, as per **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., **RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT**). **(2) Component-based structure**: The system is decomposed into **components, connectors, and interfaces**, described using **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**. **(3) Behavioral and dynamic modeling**: The system’s **behavior is explicitly modeled** using **state machines (SysML v1.7), MontiArc Automaton, or simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines [29,30]**) to capture real-time or discrete-event dynamics. **(4) Heterogeneous stakeholder integration**: The system involves **diverse stakeholders and perspectives**, requiring **interoperability across modeling notations and tools** (e.g., SysML for architecture, C++/Java for implementation, simulation tools for validation). **(5) Operational domain**: Primarily focused on **manufacturing systems**, where digital shadows (as defined by **Kritzinger et al. [32]**) are used to collect **contextual data traces** for aggregation and abstraction, but the **Digital Twin (DT) extends this by enabling **active, bidirectional data flow** for autonomous interpretation and processing. The system’s suitability for digital twinning stems from its **high complexity, dynamic behavior, and need for real-time decision-making** across physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, categorized by functionality and target stakeholders: **(1) Data Acquisition & Shadowing**: Passive collection of **one-way, automated data flows** from the physical system (as per **Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]**), including **contextual data traces** aggregated and abstracted for analysis. **(2) Active Interpretation & Processing**: Unlike digital shadows, the DT includes **active components** (e.g., **autonomous software modules**) to **interpret, analyze, and process** incoming data [35]–[37], enabling **real-time decision support**. **(3) Simulation & Behavioral Modeling**: Integration with **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines [29,30]**) to **model system behavior** using **state machines, automata (MontiArc Automaton), or discrete-event simulations**, supporting **predictive and optimization services**. **(4) Monitoring & Visualization**: **Real-time monitoring** of system states via **ADL-based models (e.g., SysML IBDs, AADL)** and **visualization tools** to provide stakeholders (e.g., engineers, operators) with **interactive dashboards or model-driven insights**. **(5) Control & Optimization**: **Closed-loop control services** leveraging **active DT components** to **optimize system performance** (e.g., via **predictive analytics, adaptive algorithms, or automated feedback loops**). **(6) Stakeholder-Specific Services**: Tailored services for **heterogeneous users**, including:
   - **Engineers**: Access to **functional/logical architecture models** (e.g., **ADLs, SysML**) for design validation.
   - **Operators**: **Real-time operational insights** via **simulation-driven visualizations**.
   - **Decision-Makers**: **Strategic optimization reports** derived from **data-driven predictions**.
   The DT’s services are **modular and extensible**, supporting **bidirectional interaction** between the physical and digital domains to enable **autonomous, data-driven operations**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** relies on a **heterogeneous toolchain** comprising the following **technologies, frameworks, and enablers**, each serving distinct roles in modeling, simulation, and data processing: **(1) Modeling & Architecture Tools**:
   - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **multi-level abstraction modeling** (requirements, functional/logical architecture, product layer) and **behavioral modeling via state machines (v1.7)**.
   - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: **ACME, AADL, MontiArc** for **component-connector-based system decomposition**, including **interface definitions** and **composition hierarchies**.
   - **MontiArc Automaton**: Specialized **state-based behavioral modeling** for dynamic systems.
   **(2) Simulation & Execution Engines**:
   - **MathWorks Simulink**: **Model-based simulation** for **real-time or discrete-event system dynamics**, integrated with **code generation (C++/Java)** for deployment.
   - **Other Simulation Tools [29,30]**: Generic or domain-specific **simulation environments** for **validation, testing, or predictive analytics**.
   **(3) Programming & Implementation**:
   - **General-Purpose Languages**: **C++/Java** for **low-level implementation** of DT components (e.g., **active data processing modules, control algorithms**).
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: **Automated code generation** from **SysML/ADL models** to reduce manual implementation errors.
   **(4) Data & Integration Layer**:
   - **Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]**: **UML-based class diagram** defining **data trace aggregation, abstraction, and one-way flow** from physical to digital.
   - **Active DT Components**: **Software modules** (e.g., **predictive analytics engines, optimization solvers**) to **process and act on data** bidirectionally.
   **(5) Interoperability & Stakeholder Integration**:
   - **Heterogeneous Tool Integration**: **Bridging gaps** between **SysML (modeling), Simulink (simulation), and C++/Java (implementation)** via **model transformation or API-based connectors**.
   - **Stakeholder-Specific Views**: **Customized interfaces** (e.g., **visualization dashboards, model explorers**) to support **diverse roles** (engineers, operators, managers).
   **(6) Enablers for Digital Twinning**:
   - **MBSE Methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT)**: **Structured approaches** to **manage complexity** across system layers.
   - **Bidirectional Data Flow**: **Active DT components** enable **feedback loops** (e.g., **control signals, optimization adjustments**) to the physical system, unlike passive **digital shadows**.
   The toolchain ensures **end-to-end digital twinning**, from **modeling and simulation** to **real-time operation and optimization**, with **scalability** for **large-scale, heterogeneous systems**.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system may involve **multiple instances** of digital twins, particularly in decentralized architectures, where each instance represents a specific physical object or subsystem. The document does not specify exact multiplicities (e.g., 1:1, 1:N, or N:M mappings) but implies a **hierarchical structure** where digital twins can be composed or coordinated. Centralized architectures may aggregate data from multiple physical objects into a single digital twin, while decentralized approaches distribute responsibilities across multiple digital twin instances. The document does not detail **synchronization frequencies** or **temporal granularity needs**, but it suggests that **active components** (e.g., simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) are required to process and interpret data flows between physical and digital entities, implying real-time or near-real-time synchronization requirements for certain use cases. No explicit latency requirements are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s **orchestration and coordination** rely on **architecture patterns** derived from **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**. These methodologies decompose system complexity into **multiple abstraction layers** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). For **component integration**, the document emphasizes the use of **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define **components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**. Behavioral descriptions are often enhanced with **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed behavior modeling may involve **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink)** or **general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)**. **Resource management** and **system-wide coordination** are implied to be handled through these integrated modeling and simulation tools, ensuring alignment between digital and physical system states. The document does not specify explicit **orchestration protocols** (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) but suggests a **heterogeneous, tool-agnostic integration** approach.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system’s **horizontal integration** with external systems is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the context implies dependencies on **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives**, requiring **interoperability standards** and **data exchange protocols** to integrate with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. While no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST APIs) or standards (e.g., ISO 23247 for Digital Twins) are mentioned, the use of **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) suggests a need for **semantic interoperability** and **data harmonization** across tools. The document does not provide concrete examples of **integration patterns** (e.g., federated Digital Twins, service-oriented architectures) or **dependencies on external systems**, but it acknowledges the challenge of managing **heterogeneous modeling notations and tools** in a cohesive Digital Twin ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system integrates **computational models and data components** across multiple abstraction layers, leveraging **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies such as **SysML** (Systems Modeling Language) for structural and behavioral descriptions. Key model types include:
  - **Geometric models** (implicitly referenced via SysML's logical/physical architecture layers),
  - **Behavioral models** (e.g., **state machines in SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton** for component interactions),
  - **Analytical models** (e.g., **Simulink** for dynamic system simulations or **general-purpose code** in C++/Java for physics-based computations).
  Data sources are heterogeneous, originating from **physical systems** (via sensors/actuators) and **external tools** (e.g., simulation environments). Data management approaches involve:
  - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, or MontiArc** to define **component-connector relationships**, interfaces, and compositions.
  - **Integration of modeling tools** (e.g., **SysML IBDs** for logical architecture, **Simulink** for behavioral validation) to ensure consistency across **requirements, functional, logical, and product layers** (e.g., via **RFLP, OOSEM, or SPES methodologies**).
  Model relationships are governed by **cross-tool synchronization** (e.g., exporting SysML models to Simulink for co-simulation) and **ontological alignment** to resolve semantic heterogeneity among stakeholders and perspectives (as noted in [31]).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system emphasizes **model fidelity** through **multi-layered validation and verification (V&V)** methods, including:
  - **Behavioral validation** via **state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton** to ensure logical consistency with physical system dynamics.
  - **Simulation-based verification** using tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or other **co-simulation environments** (e.g., [29], [30]) to quantify **uncertainty sources** (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) and **error propagation** across abstraction layers.
  - **Quality assurance processes** tied to **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., **RFLP, OOSEM**) to trace requirements through functional/logical architectures to the physical layer, ensuring **traceability of data provenance** and **model accuracy**.
  Uncertainty quantification is implicit in the **data aggregation and abstraction** process of the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1), where **contextual traces** are refined into actionable insights for the DT. Error detection relies on **cross-tool consistency checks** (e.g., comparing SysML IBDs with Simulink outputs) and **automated data flow validation** between physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. This flow involves **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs, environmental parameters) aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose. The data types transmitted are typically **heterogeneous**, including structured (e.g., time-series sensor data, metadata) and unstructured (e.g., images, logs) formats. The **communication protocols** are not explicitly specified in the document but are implied to be industry-standard protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, HTTP/REST) for industrial IoT applications. **Transmission frequencies** depend on the use case but are often **event-triggered** (e.g., threshold crossings, periodic polling) or **continuous** (e.g., real-time streaming). **Data quality assurance** mechanisms are not detailed, but preprocessing steps may include **aggregation, normalization, and abstraction** to reduce noise and extract meaningful features. **Data integration mechanisms** involve combining data from multiple sources (e.g., PLCs, SCADA, ERP systems) into a unified digital model, often using **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines** or middleware tools like **Apache Kafka** for event streaming. The document emphasizes that the digital shadow (a precursor to the DT) is **passive**, requiring external software systems to interpret and process the incoming data [33], [34].
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the context implies the need for **heterogeneous communication protocols** to support interactions between physical and digital components, likely including **industrial protocols (e.g., OPC UA, Modbus, Profibus)** for machine-to-machine communication and **standardized IoT protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP)** for lightweight, event-driven data exchange. The **network topology** would typically follow a **hybrid architecture**, combining **edge computing nodes** (for low-latency local processing) and **centralized cloud/on-premise servers** for global coordination. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are not quantified but are inferred to prioritize **real-time or near-real-time** performance for critical applications (e.g., predictive maintenance, process optimization). **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** would include **failover protocols, load balancing, and fault-tolerant designs**, though specific implementations (e.g., SDN, MPLS) are not mentioned. **Security protocols** would likely adhere to **industrial cybersecurity standards** (e.g., IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82) for authentication, encryption (e.g., TLS, DTLS), and access control, but these are not elaborated in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **deployment infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly described in the document. However, the context suggests support for **multi-tier deployment models**, including **cloud-based** (e.g., AWS IoT, Microsoft Azure Digital Twins), **edge computing** (for localized processing of high-frequency data), and **on-premise** (for sensitive or low-latency-critical applications). **Computational and storage requirements** would vary by use case but would likely involve **scalable microservices architectures** (e.g., containerized deployments using Docker/Kubernetes) to handle dynamic workloads. **Scalability mechanisms** would include **auto-scaling, elastic resource allocation, and distributed databases** (e.g., NoSQL for unstructured data). The document does not specify **availability/reliability assurance** methods, but these would typically involve **high-availability clustering, backup/recovery systems, and SLAs** tailored to the application (e.g., 99.99% uptime for mission-critical systems). The **deployment models** would need to integrate with **MBSE tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink) and **simulation environments** for validation, though the exact integration is not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process for Digital Twin (DT) systems leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structuring system architecture across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, and **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition, with extensions like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** for behavioral modeling. Integration with tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]), and general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**) ensures comprehensive system representation. **Quality assurance** and **testing** are implicitly supported by these methodologies, though specific validation approaches are not detailed. **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly addressed in the provided context, but the modular and layered approach suggests adaptability through iterative refinement of models across abstraction levels. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) implies a foundational data aggregation layer, but lifecycle governance (e.g., compliance, auditing) is not specified.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The provided context does not explicitly mention **standards compliance**, **certification requirements**, or **compliance frameworks** for Digital Twin systems. However, the use of **SysML** (ISO/IEC 19505) and **ADLs** (e.g., **AADL**, **ACME**) suggests adherence to **industry-standard modeling languages** for system architecture and behavior. **Interoperability standards** are implied through the integration of heterogeneous tools (e.g., **Simulink**, **C++/Java**), but no specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MTConnect) or certification bodies (e.g., ISO 23247 for DTs) are referenced. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) aligns with **manufacturing-specific data aggregation** but does not address broader standardization frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents outline foundational security and safety considerations for Digital Twin systems, particularly in the context of **Industrie 4.0** and **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** architectures. Key elements include:
  - **Cybersecurity Protocols**: The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** (as described in [46], [47], and [50]) integrates **authentication and authorization mechanisms** to ensure secure access to digital twin data and functionalities. The **RAMI4.0** reference architecture ([48], [49]) emphasizes **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **identity management** to enforce granular permissions across hierarchical layers of the automation pyramid ([42]).
  - **Access Control Mechanisms**: The **AAS** employs **OAuth 2.0** and **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** for secure API interactions ([50]), while **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** ([52]) is leveraged for lightweight, encrypted communication between IoT devices and digital twin components, ensuring **end-to-end security** via TLS/SSL.
  - **Threat Mitigation Strategies**: The **AAS** architecture ([46], [47]) incorporates **encryption of data at rest and in transit**, **digital signatures** for integrity verification, and **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** to monitor anomalous behavior in real-time. The **RAMI4.0** framework ([49]) mandates **cybersecurity compliance** aligned with **ISO/IEC 27001** and **IEC 62443** standards.
  - **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: In **model-driven engineering of Digital Twins** ([43]), **functional mockup interfaces (FMI)** ([51]) are used to ensure **simulation model isolation** and **failover capabilities** during runtime anomalies. The **Digital Shadow** concept ([42]) introduces **redundant data synchronization** across automation pyramid layers to maintain operational continuity.
  - **Risk Assessment Approaches**: The **AAS** ([50]) supports **risk-based asset management** by integrating **threat modeling** and **vulnerability assessments** into its metadata structure. The **RAMI4.0** ([49]) aligns with **IEC 61508** for **functional safety** in industrial systems, ensuring compliance with **safety integrity levels (SIL)**.
  - **Safety-Critical System Considerations**: For **robotics applications** (e.g., [44], [45]), the **Digital Twin** incorporates **real-time friction modeling** ([45]) and **dynamics learning** to preemptively identify **safety-critical states** (e.g., collision risks). The **AAS** ([46]) enables **safety certification** by embedding **machine-readable safety certificates** and **compliance proofs** into asset metadata.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
